section.title: The Idols of Babylon Fall
section.sectionRationale: This chapter draws the polemic against idolatry to its sharpest picture yet: Babylon's own gods, Bel and Nebo, become dead weight loaded onto exhausted animals in a desperate escape, while the LORD declares that He alone has carried Israel from the womb and will carry them still to old age. It presses home the Book of Comfort's central claim, that only the LORD declares the end from the beginning and His purpose cannot be stopped.
section.thesis: Babylon's idols must be carried away as dead weight by animals that stagger under their burden, but the LORD has carried Israel from the womb and promises to carry them to old age, because He alone declares the end from the beginning and no one can prevent His purpose from standing.
section.frameNote: Five movements: Babylon's gods collapse and are hauled away; the LORD's contrasting promise to carry His people from birth to old age; the folly of comparing God to a crafted idol; the LORD's declaration that His purpose will stand, unlike anything the idols can foretell; and the closing call for stubborn hearts to remember and draw near to His righteousness.

verses.text: Bel crouches; Nebo cowers. Their idols weigh down beasts and cattle. The images you carry are burdensome, a load to the weary animal.
The gods cower; they crouch together, unable to relieve the burden; but they themselves go into captivity.

units.label: I have carried you since birth

verses.text: “Listen to Me, O house of Jacob, all the remnant of the house of Israel, who have been sustained from the womb, carried along since birth.
Even to your old age, I will be the same, and I will bear you up when you turn gray. I have made you, and I will {{nasa|carry}} you; I will sustain you and deliver you.

units.label: To whom will you liken Me?

verses.text: To whom will you liken Me or count Me equal? To whom will you compare Me, that we should be alike?
They pour out their bags of gold and weigh out silver on scales; they hire a goldsmith to fashion it into a god, so they can bow down and worship.

They lift it to their shoulder and carry it along; they set it in its place, and there it stands, not budging from that spot. They cry out to it, but it does not answer; it saves no one from his troubles.

units.label: My purpose will stand
units.note: declaring the end from the beginning

verses.text: Remember this and be brave; take it to heart, you transgressors!
Remember what happened long ago, for I am God, and there is no other; I am God, and there is none like Me.
I declare the end from the beginning, and ancient times from what is still to come. I say, ‘My {{etsah|purpose}} will stand, and all My good pleasure I will accomplish.’
I summon a bird of prey from the east, a man for My purpose from a far-off land. Truly I have spoken, and truly I will bring it to pass. I have planned it, and I will surely do it.
Listen to Me, you stubborn people, far removed from righteousness:
I am bringing My righteousness near; it is not far away, and My salvation will not be delayed. I will grant salvation to Zion and adorn Israel with My splendor.

nasa.gloss: To carry, lift, bear, or bear up. The chapter's whole argument turns on this word: Babylon's idols must be carried as dead weight by exhausted animals, while the LORD carries His own people from the womb to old age, bearing their weight rather than being borne, a contrast that anticipates the ultimate bearing of sin itself at the cross, 1 Peter 2:24.

etsah.gloss: Counsel, purpose, or plan, the settled intention behind a course of action. The LORD's declaration that His etsah will stand is the theological center of the chapter, and indeed of the whole Book of Comfort: no idol, no empire, and no circumstance can prevent God's declared purpose from being accomplished exactly as He has spoken.

basic.context: This chapter pictures Babylon's chief gods, Bel and Nebo, being loaded like dead weight onto exhausted pack animals as their city falls, unable even to save themselves, let alone their worshipers. Against that image the LORD reminds Israel that He has carried them from the womb and will carry them still into old age, because He alone declares the future and His purpose cannot be stopped by anyone.
basic.hermeneutics: The vivid image of idols becoming a burden rather than a help exposes the core absurdity Isaiah has been building across these chapters: an idol must be carried, while the true God carries. The chapter's declaration in verse 10, that the LORD declares the end from the beginning, is the theological anchor for the whole Book of Comfort's confidence that God's promises, including the naming of Cyrus in the previous chapter, will certainly come to pass.
basic.originalLanguages: The whole chapter turns on the contrast built into {{nasa}}, to carry, since Babylon's idols must be carried as burdensome weight while the LORD promises to carry His people from birth to old age. That promise rests on His {{etsah}}, purpose or counsel, declared unshakeable, standing firm against every rival claim to control the future.
basic.typology: The LORD who carries His people rather than needing to be carried anticipates Christ, who does not merely sustain His people from a distance but takes on flesh to personally bear their sins in His own body, 1 Peter 2:24. The declaration that God's purpose will stand and His good pleasure will be accomplished, verse 10, anticipates the certainty of God's redemptive plan fulfilled in Christ, unshaken by any human or demonic opposition.
basic.christ: The contrast between idols that must be carried and the LORD who carries His people finds its deepest fulfillment in Christ, who 'Himself bore our sins in His body on the tree,' 1 Peter 2:24, carrying not merely His people's weight through life but the very weight of their sin at the cross. The declaration that God's purpose will stand and His good pleasure will be accomplished, verse 10, is the same confidence Paul expresses regarding God's eternal plan centered in Christ, Ephesians 1:11.
basic.god: God alone is able to carry His people rather than needing to be carried, sustaining them from the womb to old age with the same faithfulness across a whole lifetime. His purpose cannot be stopped by any rival power, human or supposedly divine, because He alone declares the end from the beginning and brings to pass exactly what He has spoken.
basic.crossReferences: Deuteronomy 32:11-12 pictures the LORD carrying Israel like an eagle bearing its young, similar carrying imagery. 1 Peter 2:24 describes Christ bearing our sins in His own body on the cross. Ephesians 1:11 affirms that God works all things according to the purpose of His will. Acts 15:18 declares that all God's works have been known to Him from eternity.
basic.oneStory: This chapter's contrast between burdensome idols and the God who carries His people traces the whole Bible's story of a God who does not merely watch from a distance but personally sustains and ultimately rescues His people. That carrying finds its deepest expression in Christ, who bore the full weight of sin itself, fulfilling the unstoppable purpose this chapter declares God will accomplish.

context.literary: Structured in five compact movements, opening with a vivid narrative image of collapsing idols, moving through the LORD's contrasting promise and His challenge to compare Him to any idol, and closing with His declaration that His purpose will stand and salvation is near.
context.historical: Bel, also known as Marduk, and Nebo, or Nabu, were among Babylon's chief deities; this chapter anticipates the city's fall to Cyrus, when its idols, far from protecting the city, would themselves need to be evacuated or would be captured, exactly the humiliation described here.
context.canonical: Continues directly from the naming of Cyrus in chapters 44 to 45, reinforcing that same argument, only the LORD can declare the end from the beginning, and prepares for the extended taunt against Babylon's fall in the following chapter.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To expose the absurdity of trusting idols that must themselves be carried away as dead weight, and to assure God's people that the LORD who has carried them since birth, and who alone can declare the future, will certainly accomplish His purpose for them.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a specific historical humiliation of Babylon's gods and a specific assurance to exiled Israel; it is not a general claim that every hardship will resolve identically. Its abiding principle is that God's declared purposes cannot be thwarted by any rival power, and that He personally sustains His people across their whole lives.

debates.question: Does 'My purpose will stand, and all My good pleasure I will accomplish,' verse 10, describe God's meticulous control over every individual event, or His unstoppable overarching plan?

views.label: God's meticulous, comprehensive sovereignty
views.case: The strongest reading takes this declaration to mean God ordains and directs every detail of history, so that nothing, down to the smallest event, falls outside His decreed purpose.

views.label: God's overarching redemptive plan is certain, without specifying every mechanism
views.case: A narrower reading takes the verse to guarantee that God's ultimate purposes for His people and for history's outcome cannot be frustrated, without necessarily settling debated questions about the precise relationship between divine sovereignty and human choice in every individual event.

debates.bearing: Christians across traditions differ on the finer mechanics of providence, but this verse's plain comfort for its first hearers, and for readers now, is that no power can stop God's declared purpose of redemption from being accomplished, a truth that should steady faith regardless of how one resolves the deeper philosophical question.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter's central contrast rests on {{nasa}}, to carry, since Babylon's idols must be carried as dead weight by staggering animals while the LORD promises to carry His own people from the womb to old age. That promise is anchored in His {{etsah}}, purpose or counsel, which He declares will stand unshaken against every rival claim on the future.

typology.type: The LORD who carries His people rather than needing to be carried
typology.fulfillment: Christ, who personally bore His people's sins in His own body on the cross, 1 Peter 2:24.

typology.type: The declaration that God's purpose will stand and His good pleasure will be accomplished
typology.fulfillment: God's eternal redemptive plan, centered in Christ, that cannot be frustrated, Ephesians 1:11.

study.christ: The contrast at the heart of this chapter, idols that must be carried against the LORD who carries His own people, reaches its fullest expression in Christ, who 'Himself bore our sins in His body on the tree,' 1 Peter 2:24, carrying not merely His people's weight through life but the crushing weight of their sin at the cross. The declaration that God's purpose will stand and His good pleasure will be accomplished, verse 10, echoes the same certainty Paul affirms regarding God's eternal plan fulfilled in Christ, Ephesians 1:11.
study.god: God alone carries His people rather than needing to be carried, sustaining them with the same faithfulness from the womb to old age. No rival power can prevent His declared purpose from standing, because He alone declares the end from the beginning and brings to pass exactly what He has spoken, unlike any idol that stands mute and helpless when called upon.

crossReferences.note: Pictures the LORD carrying Israel like an eagle bearing its young on its wings, similar carrying imagery to this chapter.
Describes Christ bearing His people's sins in His own body on the cross, the fullest expression of the LORD who carries rather than needs carrying.
Affirms that God works all things according to the purpose of His will, echoing this chapter's declaration that His purpose will stand.
Declares that all God's works have been known to Him from eternity, echoing His claim to declare the end from the beginning.

study.oneStory: This chapter's contrast between burdensome idols and the God who carries His people traces the whole Bible's story of a God who does not merely observe from a distance but personally sustains and ultimately rescues His people. That carrying reaches its deepest expression in Christ, who bore the full weight of sin itself, fulfilling the unstoppable purpose this chapter declares God will certainly accomplish.

quiz.q: What happens to Babylon's gods, Bel and Nebo, in verses 1 to 2?

options.text: They become a burdensome load carried by exhausted animals and are themselves taken into captivity.
options.note: Verses 1 to 2 describe exactly this humiliating scene of idols needing rescue rather than providing it.

options.text: They successfully defend Babylon from all attackers.
options.note: The opposite; the idols collapse and must be evacuated, unable to save the city or themselves.

options.text: They are worshiped with even greater devotion after this event.
options.note: The passage depicts humiliation and captivity for these idols, not increased devotion.

options.text: They are melted down and used to build new temples.
options.note: The text describes them being carried away as a burden into captivity, not repurposed for new temples.

quiz.q: What contrasting promise does the LORD make about Israel in verses 3 to 4?

options.text: He has carried them from the womb and will bear them up even to old age.
options.note: Verses 3 to 4 state this promise directly, contrasting with the idols that must be carried.

options.text: He will abandon them once they reach old age.
options.note: The opposite; the promise explicitly extends His carrying to old age and beyond.

options.text: He requires Israel to carry Him, just as Babylon carries its idols.
options.note: The whole point of the contrast is that the LORD carries His people, unlike idols that must be carried.

options.text: He will only help Israel if they first prove their strength.
options.note: The promise is unconditional care from the womb onward, not contingent on Israel's own strength.

quiz.q: What does verse 7 say about an idol's ability to respond to those who cry out to it?

options.text: It does not answer and saves no one from his troubles.
options.note: Verse 7 states this directly, the core indictment against idol worship.

options.text: It answers immediately and rescues its worshipers.
options.note: The opposite; the text emphasizes the idol's total inability to answer or save.

options.text: It answers only the wealthiest worshipers.
options.note: The text describes total inability to answer anyone, not selective responsiveness based on wealth.

options.text: It answers only after elaborate rituals are performed.
options.note: The text gives no indication of eventual response after ritual; it states the idol simply does not answer at all.

quiz.q: What does the LORD declare about Himself uniquely in verse 10?

options.text: He declares the end from the beginning, and His purpose will stand.
options.note: Verse 10 states this unique claim directly, central to the whole chapter's argument.

options.text: He is uncertain about the future, like the idols.
options.note: The opposite; He claims certain, comprehensive knowledge and control of the future, unlike any idol.

options.text: His plans frequently change and can be altered by other gods.
options.note: The verse specifically claims His purpose will stand, not that it is subject to change or alteration.

options.text: He only knows the past, not the future.
options.note: The claim explicitly includes declaring 'the end from the beginning,' encompassing the future, not only the past.

quiz.q: What does the LORD promise in the chapter's closing verse, verse 13?

options.text: He is bringing His righteousness near and His salvation will not be delayed, granting salvation to Zion.
options.note: Verse 13 gives exactly this closing promise of near, certain salvation.

options.text: Salvation will be indefinitely postponed with no clear timeline.
options.note: The opposite; the verse specifically says salvation 'will not be delayed.'

options.text: Only future generations will experience this promised salvation.
options.note: The language emphasizes nearness and lack of delay, not a promise deferred only to distant future generations.

options.text: Righteousness and salvation depend entirely on Israel's own effort.
options.note: The verse presents this as God's own initiative, 'I am bringing,' not something earned by Israel's effort.

questions: Babylon's idols became a burden that had to be carried, while the LORD carries His own people. What are you currently carrying, trying to manage or fix, that you need to let God carry instead?
God promises to sustain His people from the womb to old age, not just in youth or strength. How does this shape the way you think about aging, weakness, or seasons of life that feel less productive?
The idol in verse 7 cannot answer or save anyone who cries out to it. What do you turn to that, if you are honest, cannot actually answer you when you call?

God declares that His purpose will stand and nothing can stop it. What current uncertainty in your life do you need to entrust to that unshakeable purpose rather than trying to control the outcome yourself?
The chapter closes with the promise that God's salvation will not be delayed. Where do you need to trust God's timing rather than assuming He has forgotten or delayed His promises to you?

translationNotes.note: Bel, another name for Marduk, and Nebo, or Nabu, were principal deities of the Babylonian pantheon, making their humiliation here a direct challenge to Babylon's own religious confidence.

This verse, 'I declare the end from the beginning,' is the theological anchor for the whole Book of Comfort's confidence that God's specific promises, including the naming of Cyrus in the preceding chapters, will certainly be fulfilled.
'A bird of prey from the east' most likely continues the imagery applied to Cyrus in the surrounding chapters, a swift, decisive conqueror summoned by God for His own purposes.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture tool for Isaiah 46:1-13. No em or en dashes appeared in the source text for this passage.
